top_risk: Per-user data isolation (IDOR) at the read-only API boundary: identity must be derived server-side from the validated token and every query scoped to that Discord user, or swapping a user/guild id leaks others' stats.
cheapest_evidence: Throwaway spike: OAuth callback + one token-scoped endpoint, then run the IDOR attack (swap Discord user/guild id) and a forged/replayed state param, and confirm both are rejected server-side.
report:
# Probe — SuperBot per-server personal stats page (Phase 3 / OAuth trust-gate)

(Grounded in control/inbox.md INTAKE 002; source idea superbot-site-stats-data-story-2026-07-10.md. Front-of-pipeline probe, not the verdict.)

## 1. What this really is / core claim
Two fused claims: (a) buildability — because prod Postgres is production data, a separate superbot-lane, read-only, token-scoped API serving a versioned data contract is the only sanctioned path for the site to show a user their own XP/coins/mining/leaderboard rank; and (b) trust — the 6-item owner gate holds under adversarial verification. Net claim: buildable-as-designed AND trustworthy. Pattern is standard; novelty is entirely whether the isolation boundary is enforced in the right place. Phases 1-2 carry no auth surface and are explicitly not gated.

## 2. Strongest failure mode
Not the OAuth handshake (standard). Load-bearing risk is per-user data isolation at the API/data-contract boundary — IDOR. If authorization is enforced at the website layer after login rather than the API deriving caller identity from a validated token and scoping every query server-side, swapping the user id (or guild id for leaderboard) leaks other users' stats. The guilds scope + per-server leaderboard rank compounds this: the API must independently verify the caller is a member of the server whose leaderboard they read. Secondary: 'no long-lived token storage' interacts with the API needing some credential — where the service-to-service/session token lives, its TTL and scope: not measured. Tertiary: leaderboard rank inherently reads across users and needs its own isolation argument.

## 3. What would falsify it / cheapest adequate evidence
Falsifier: any single gate item where a concrete attack succeeds. Cheapest evidence: a narrow measured spike — throwaway OAuth callback + ONE token-scoped stats endpoint, then run: (i) IDOR — request another user's/guild's stats with a valid session, confirm server-side rejection; (ii) CSRF/state — forge and replay the callback state param, confirm rejection; (iii) scope — confirm only identify+guilds requested. Non-runnable design items get structured JUDGMENT-ONLY, labeled as such. Output = per-gate-item pass/fail checklist.

## 4. Cost/complexity vs payoff
Verification cost: S-M (adversarial checklist + narrow attack spikes, no long-running sim). Build cost: heaviest of the three phases — a separate read-only API service + versioned contract + website page + OAuth infra. Tension: building an entire separate API service purely to avoid touching prod Postgres is the correct safety call but disproportionate if the sole consumer is one stats page — payoff justification leans on the contract being reusable by future features (not measured). Payoff of the page: real but non-urgent, explicitly not blocking Phases 1-2.

## 5. Smallest shippable slice
Ship Phases 1 and 2 first — zero auth surface, deliver most of the data-story value at no trust risk. For Phase 3: one server, one user, own XP+coins only — drop mining detail and (critically) leaderboard rank from the first slice, since leaderboard is the one feature that reads across users and carries the guild-membership-verification risk. Minimal API = single token-scoped endpoint deriving identity from validated session, scoping query server-side; prove IDOR + state/CSRF rejection before adding the cross-user leaderboard read.

## Recommendation
BUILD with two hard conditions: (1) Phases 1-2 ship independently and now; (2) public launch of Phase 3 gated on the isolation + CSRF spike passing. Confidence 0.7: high that pattern is buildable, lower that it survives 'as-designed with zero changes' — most likely honest outcome is buildable-with-named-changes (server-side identity derivation + guild-membership check made explicit). Nothing is a KILL. Not measured: token storage location/TTL/scope, whether the versioned contract has consumers beyond this page, actual state/CSRF implementation.
